skill.name: yescan-ocr-universal
skill.description: Extract and structure text from images, screenshots, photos, scanned documents, IDs, invoices, tables, formulas, medical reports, and product images using the Quark Scan OCR service.
skill.summary: Use Quark Scan OCR to convert images and scanned documents into text or structured fields.
skill.author: yescan-ai

security_audit.summary: Static findings are partly confirmed: the skill intentionally runs Python commands, reads local image files, stores an API key in a hidden home file, and sends image data plus the API key to a third-party OCR endpoint. Many obfuscation and weak-crypto matches are false positives from normal imports, magic-byte checks, and base64 validation. No confirmed malicious intent or audit-targeting prompt injection was found, but the command-template and third-party data-flow risks require remediation before publication.

high_findings.title: Shell Command Template Uses User-Controlled Values
high_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to execute python3 commands after replacing IMAGE_URL, IMAGE_FILE_PATH, or IMAGE_BASE64 with user-provided values. The script validates inputs only after shell parsing, so unsafe substitution can create command-injection risk in agents that paste values into a shell command.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command templates directly include user-controlled placeholders and instruct execution. The risk depends on the agent shell invocation, so this is high confidence but not proof of malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Images and API Key Sent to Third-Party OCR Service
high_findings.description: The skill sends image URLs or base64 image data with aiApiKey to https://scan-business.quark.cn/vision. This matches the OCR purpose and is disclosed, but it exposes potentially sensitive IDs, invoices, medical reports, and the service key to an external processor.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint, request body, and privacy notice all confirm the data flow. It is intentional OCR behavior, but the affected documents and credential make the risk material.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext API Key Stored in Hidden Home File
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends writing SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY to ~/.yescan_env, and the code reads that hidden file when the environment variable is absent. This is a legitimate configuration pattern, but it stores a service credential in plaintext in the user home directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setup instructions and loader explicitly use the hidden file. The risk is credential exposure from local file permissions, not credential theft by itself.

medium_findings.title: Local File Reads Are Uploaded to External OCR
medium_findings.description: When a local path is supplied, the code expands the path, validates extension and size, reads the file, base64-encodes it, and submits it to the OCR API. This is expected for OCR, but an agent tricked into using a sensitive image path would exfiltrate that file content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload path is clear and constrained to allowed image extensions and a size limit. The concern remains because the destination is external and the file path may be user supplied.

medium_findings.title: Service-Returned Base64 Can Be Written to Temporary Files
medium_findings.description: The result handler can save base64 image data returned by the OCR service into the system temporary directory. The written format is validated as an image, but the content originates from an external service and creates local files automatically for translation outputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write path is real, but it is limited to supported image formats and generated filenames. This is moderate risk rather than direct arbitrary file write.

low_findings.title: Static Dynamic-Import Findings Are Ordinary Imports
low_findings.description: The reported dynamic import locations are normal package import statements and re-exports. No evidence found of runtime import construction or loading untrusted module names.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static Python imports. They do not use importlib, __import__, eval, or user-controlled module names.

low_findings.title: Base64, Hex, and Weak-Crypto Matches Are Validation Logic
low_findings.description: The base64 decode and hex-byte findings are used to validate input and detect image magic bytes. No evidence found of encrypted payload hiding or weak cryptographic hashing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The decoded bytes are checked against known image signatures or used to validate OCR input. The code does not use these operations to conceal executable payloads.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Service and Help Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the documented Quark Scan service endpoint and developer portal. No evidence found of unrelated webhook, paste, or data-drop endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same domain is documented in the skill metadata and user instructions. The URLs still support the confirmed network risk, but they are not standalone malicious indicators.

dangerous_patterns.title: User Input Interpolated Into Executed Shell Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The SKILL.md execution guide tells the agent to replace command placeholders with real user image values and execute the command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is in the agent instructions rather than a Python subprocess call. It is still relevant because the skill depends on shell command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Image Payload Included in External POST
dangerous_patterns.description: The request body includes aiApiKey plus dataBase64 or dataUrl and posts it to the configured OCR endpoint with redirects enabled.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The request construction and HTTP POST are explicit. The pattern is expected for OCR, but it is a sensitive external data flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Hidden Credential File
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents and reads ~/.yescan_env as a plaintext location for SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential storage flow is explicit and reproducible. The risk depends on local file permissions and user environment.

content.user_title: Extract Text From Documents and Images
content.value_statement: Teams need reliable text extraction from photos, screenshots, scans, IDs, invoices, tables, formulas, and reports. This skill routes each image to Quark Scan OCR and returns raw recognized text or structured document fields.
actual_capabilities: Recognizes text from image URLs, local image files, or base64 image input.
Selects OCR scenes for handwriting, tables, IDs, invoices, tickets, formulas, reports, and licenses.
Sends requests to the Quark Scan OCR API using SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY.
Returns the OCR service response without rewriting or summarizing the result.
Validates local image file size and common image extensions before upload.
Can save translated image output to a temporary local file for the pic-translate scene.
limitations: Requires a valid SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY and network access to the Quark Scan service.
Uploads image content to a third-party OCR processor, including sensitive document images.
Supports one static image per call and rejects local files larger than 5 MB.
Does not generate, edit, or analyze images beyond OCR and supported scan scenes.

use_cases.title: Digitize Office Documents
use_cases.description: Extract text from scanned forms, tables, receipts, and reports for review or data entry.

use_cases.title: Capture Finance Fields
use_cases.description: Read invoice, train ticket, and commercial invoice images to speed reimbursement and record checks.

use_cases.title: Process Identity Documents
use_cases.description: Recognize key fields from supported identity, license, travel permit, and business registration images.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Basic Text
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract all readable text from this image and return the OCR result exactly as provided.

prompt_templates.title: Recognize a Table
prompt_templates.prompt: Use table OCR on this image and preserve the table structure in the returned result.

prompt_templates.title: Read an Invoice
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the key fields from this invoice image, including dates, parties, line items, and amounts.

prompt_templates.title: Select the Best OCR Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Identify the document type in this image, choose the matching OCR scene, and return the raw service result.

output_examples.input: A photo of a handwritten meeting note.
output_examples.output: The recognized handwriting text is returned with the service status and any available structured fields.

output_examples.input: A screenshot of a spreadsheet-style table.
output_examples.output: The table content is returned in a structured layout that preserves rows and columns when supported.
Invoice fields such as seller, buyer, date, item details, and amount are returned when the service detects them.

best_practices: Review privacy requirements before uploading IDs, invoices, medical reports, or other sensitive images.
Use the most specific OCR scene for the document type to improve recognition quality.
Store the API key with restricted file permissions or a secure environment manager.
anti_patterns: Do not upload confidential images unless third-party OCR processing is approved.
Do not paste untrusted values into shell commands without safe argument handling.
Do not use this skill for video streams, batch jobs, or files above the documented size limit.

faq.question: Does this skill need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY from the Quark Scan developer service.

faq.question: Where does the image data go?
faq.answer: The skill sends image URLs or base64 image data to the Quark Scan OCR endpoint for processing.

faq.question: Can it read local image files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can read supported local image files up to 5 MB and upload their base64 content.

faq.question: Can it handle identity cards and invoices?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes scenes for supported identity documents, licenses, invoices, tickets, and business documents.

faq.question: Does it edit or generate images?
faq.answer: No. It is intended for OCR, document recognition, and supported scan service outputs only.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive documents?
faq.answer: Use caution. Sensitive images are processed by a third-party service and should require user approval.
